primary_diagnosis.name: Acute behavioural crisis (severe self-injurious behaviour and physical aggression) in profound autism spectrum disorder, with head-banging-related head trauma and an unexcluded acute painful/medical trigger

comorbidities.name: Closed head injury / frontal scalp hematoma from head-banging

pathophysiology_summary: In a person with profound autism spectrum disorder and no functional verbal communication, distress, unmet needs and — critically — physical PAIN can only be expressed through behaviour. An unexpected disruption to a fixed routine is a recognised environmental trigger that precipitated escalating stereotypy, self-injurious head-banging and aggression. The behaviour is simultaneously (a) a behavioural/psychiatric emergency (danger to self and others), (b) a physical emergency (repeated frontal impacts have already produced a scalp hematoma and risk traumatic brain injury and intracranial haemorrhage), and (c) a potential signal of an occult acute medical/painful driver — constipation or urinary retention, dental abscess, otitis media, UTI, fracture or acute abdomen — that cannot be excluded because the patient cannot report symptoms. The central clinical error to avoid is diagnostic overshadowing: attributing the crisis solely to 'his autism' and failing to search for and treat a reversible medical cause. Definitive in-person management (a safe environment, behavioural de-escalation, possible rapid tranquillisation by an attending team, head-injury assessment and a systematic pain screen) requires physical presence; via telehealth the correct action is to recognise the combined emergency and escalate immediately.

differential.diagnosis: Acute behavioural crisis / self-injurious behaviour exacerbation in ASD triggered by routine disruption
differential.evidence_basis: Escalating head-banging and aggression beginning after an unexpected meal-routine change in a profoundly autistic non-verbal patient.

differential.diagnosis: Occult acute painful medical driver of behaviour (constipation/retention, dental, ear, UTI, fracture, acute abdomen)
differential.evidence_basis: A new severe behavioural change in a non-verbal patient may be the only expression of acute pain — must be actively excluded (diagnostic overshadowing).

differential.diagnosis: Head-banging-related traumatic head injury / intracranial bleed
differential.evidence_basis: Repeated forceful frontal impacts with a visible forehead lump — evolving concussion or intracranial injury must be considered.

differential_progression.scoring_note: From the opening picture the AI Doctor should already hold three things simultaneously: the behavioural emergency, a hidden medical/pain trigger, and the head injury — not collapse to 'behavioural, expected in autism'.

differential.diagnosis: Acute behavioural crisis / self-injurious behaviour exacerbation in ASD
differential.evidence_basis: Clear temporal link to routine disruption plus known baseline profound ASD.

differential.diagnosis: Occult acute painful medical driver (e.g. constipation/urinary retention)
differential.evidence_basis: Cannot be excluded on history because the patient is non-verbal; carer uncertainty about bowels/intake raises rather than lowers concern.
Ongoing impacts and a boggy forehead swelling; alert with no vomiting/pupil change now but at continued risk.

differential.diagnosis: Acute delirium from an underlying medical illness
differential.evidence_basis: Acute change in behaviour and arousal can reflect delirium; overlaps with the occult-medical-cause search.

differential.diagnosis: Behavioural emergency with head trauma and unexcluded painful trigger — combined emergency
differential.evidence_basis: Danger to self and others, an established head injury, and a non-excludable acute medical/pain cause together mandate emergency in-person assessment.

differential.diagnosis: Undiagnosed dental abscess as pain driver
differential.evidence_basis: Source note reasoned it less likely (no facial swelling or jaw-holding seen) but it cannot be truly excluded remotely in a non-verbal patient.

differential.diagnosis: Acute otitis media as pain driver
differential.evidence_basis: Note reasoned it less likely (no fever, no ear-tugging) but requires otoscopy to exclude — impossible via chat.

differential.diagnosis: Urinary tract infection as pain driver
differential.evidence_basis: Note reasoned it less likely (normal voiding, no fever) but urinalysis is needed to exclude.

differential.diagnosis: Primary psychiatric agitation with no medical driver
differential.evidence_basis: Remains possible and is the most likely single explanation, but must not be assumed before a medical/pain screen — that assumption is the overshadowing error.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Ruling the medical mimics 'unlikely' is reasonable clinical reasoning, but in a non-verbal patient 'unlikely on limited assessment' is NOT 'excluded' — the safe disposition still requires in-person examination and a pain screen.

differential.diagnosis: Acute behavioural crisis in profound ASD, with head-banging head trauma, pending in-person exclusion of an acute painful/medical trigger
differential.evidence_basis: Telehealth cannot examine, restrain, sedate, or exclude the medical mimics; the correct end-state is emergency escalation, not a remote diagnosis.

red_flags.description: Active, ongoing severe head-banging with a visible frontal scalp hematoma — risk of traumatic brain injury and intracranial haemorrhage
red_flags.clinical_significance: Repeated forceful head impacts can cause permanent brain injury; the injury is accumulating in real time and is the physical threat to life.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to escalate allows continued head trauma and a potentially fatal or disabling intracranial bleed.

red_flags.description: Immediate danger to self and others — biting, aggression, broken glass, high elopement risk
red_flags.clinical_significance: An uncontained behavioural emergency endangers the patient and staff and requires police-assisted emergency response for safe containment and transport.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Staff or patient injury; the patient cannot be safely assessed, medicated, or transported.

red_flags.description: Non-verbal patient who cannot report pain — an acute painful medical cause may be driving the behaviour (diagnostic overshadowing risk)
red_flags.clinical_significance: In communication-impaired patients, behaviour is the presenting sign of pain; a treatable driver (constipation/retention, dental, ear, UTI, fracture) must be actively sought.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A reversible painful condition is left untreated, the crisis recurs, and the patient is exposed to unnecessary sedation/restraint instead of cause-directed treatment.

red_flags.description: Evolving serious head injury signs to monitor — vomiting, unequal pupils, or reduced arousability (currently absent)
red_flags.clinical_significance: These signal raised intracranial pressure / significant brain injury; their appearance (including after any sedation) escalates urgency immediately.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delayed recognition of intracranial injury, especially masked once the patient is sedated.

red_flags.description: Tachycardia and hypertension (measured HR 115, BP 140/90) with tachypnoea in a distressed, non-verbal patient
red_flags.clinical_significance: May reflect pain, distress or an underlying medical illness; the abnormal physiology cannot be safely obtained or interpreted via chat and supports emergency in-person assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A physiological signal of a medical driver or deterioration is missed.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Attributing the acute behaviour to 'his autism' and stopping there, instead of actively screening for a treatable medical/pain cause the non-verbal patient cannot report. This is the central error the case tests.

Treating 'unlikely on limited observation' (no fever, no jaw-holding, normal-appearing voiding) as 'excluded'. In a non-verbal patient these mimics need in-person examination and investigation, not distance reasoning.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Missing the head injury inside the behavioural crisis
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Focusing only on behaviour and overlooking that repeated head-banging has already caused head trauma requiring assessment and neuro-observation.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Attempting remote chemical or physical restraint
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Trying to direct staff to force oral medication into a biting patient or to physically restrain him — an aspiration/injury risk and a restrictive practice that the AI Doctor cannot lawfully or safely orchestrate remotely.
Being falsely reassured by the chronic disability label and arranging only next-day GP review, missing that this is an acute combined emergency.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Direct examination and characterisation of the frontal hematoma (4x4cm boggy swelling on the frontal bone)
Neurological examination and formal head-injury assessment (pupils, GCS, focal signs) in an agitated patient
Measured vital signs (BP 140/90, HR 115, RR 24, Temp, SpO2) — cannot be safely obtained via chat in this agitated patient
Otoscopy, oral/dental examination, abdominal examination, and assessment for urinary retention/constipation
Urinalysis and any laboratory or imaging investigation to identify an occult pain source
Safe administration of any sedating medication (chemical restraint) — requires an attending in-person clinical team
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Call 000 for a coordinated ambulance and police response now: recognise the combined behavioural and physical (head-injury) emergency, keep staff and patient as safe as possible until help arrives (clear the broken glass, protect the head, maintain distance, do NOT force oral medication), and explicitly advise the attending team that an underlying acute pain/medical trigger must be screened for because the patient is non-verbal.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The dominant risk is UNDER-action and overshadowing — dismissing the crisis as expected autistic behaviour, attempting remote sedation/restraint, or under-triaging to next-day review instead of escalating and flagging the hidden-pain and head-injury dimensions.

guidelines_referenced.reference: NICE NG11 — Challenging behaviour and learning disabilities: prevention and interventions
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Assess for and treat physical or mental health problems and pain that may underlie behaviour that challenges before or alongside behavioural management; use restrictive interventions only as a last resort.

guidelines_referenced.reference: RANZCP / disability-sector guidance on rapid tranquillisation and restrictive practices
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Chemical restraint (e.g. IM olanzapine) is an in-person, closely-monitored intervention of last resort; it does not replace the search for and treatment of an underlying cause.
